text: No, actually, quite the opposite: It's important for VLN that they did not make an exception for Max and had him go through the exact same hoops and the exact same decision making progress as everyone else. No doubt Max is the best closed circuit racer in the world at the moment and will not struggle to get safely around the track, but he has absolutely zero experience in multi class (non-i)Racing and wanted to partake in the most difficult multi class race in the world. text: I remember an interview with Patrick Head where he talked about getting their first engine from Honda This would have been in '83, as they were building their first Honda-powered car for '84 (which they actually ran in the final race of '83) He said that an engine just arrived in a box from Japan No data sheets, nothing, just an engine He needed heat rejection data so they could design and specify the cooling system for the hot turbo engine (up to this point the team had been running Cosworth DFV). Obviously there needed to be adequate cooling to keep the engine reliable, but not too much as that added weight (radiators too big etc) So he asked Honda and they just said "work it out for yourself" It was acceptable in the 80s, but not 40+ years later

text: Did you account for repeated measures in your MANOVA? If you have qualifying position and race result both as dependent variables in the same model (which you don't specify, but you only list one MANOVA p-value) , you have to account for the fact that one may have an effect on the other, so you have to have a repeated measures correction using something like mixed effects models in lme4. That could affect significance.
post_title: A response to the Bearman-beef correlation post

text: I highly doubt he's doing a multivariate linear mixed effects model for this... Even if he is, the LMM would have to rely quite a bit on partial pooling which would shrink the estimates quite severely since the data is pretty limited. I'm not even going to get into the assumptions of this model including normality, constant variance (with a Frequentist framework even modelling sigma is hard and pretty much impossible with this amount of data, etc. It's a meme analysis but I always get concerned since a ton of poeple may not understand that.
post_title: A response to the Bearman-beef correlation post

text: Yeah, I'm just pointing out that qualifying and race result are almost certainly related results. A MANOVA requires that they be related only in their dependence on the independent variable, and you have to account for that if they are or the results aren't reliable. A repeated measures ANOVA would probably be a better test for this because of the issues you pointed out.

text: ot sure why they didn’t focus on the car as much. Maybe politics. Maybe Max’s results clouded their minds and the collective consensus was “it’s about a bad driver”. It's probably two things. The first is - as you mentioned - when one driver performs well it's hard to say "car is shit, fix". Even if you grant that Max is an alien, you still have some lingering doubt that makes you think "it can't be that shit" and you can't really accept that one driver wins the race from pole while the other struggles to overtake alpines and Haas. The second thing is that even accepting that the car is problematic, this doesn't mean you can fix it. Building an F1 car is tough, let alone a fast and easy to drive one. With the cost cap and a very rough aero regulation in place it's even tougher. F1 teams are always obviously working to make the car better - whether drivers are struggling or not - but that doesn't mean they'll succeed and the failure of the second Red Bull seat doesn't point against that because the cars have usually improved over time. With all that it's easy to see why Red Bull decide to just focus on Max and run through second drivers.

text: I don't want to completely write off the level of success that Red Bull enjoyed during that period (particularly 2023), but let's also acknowledge that this isn't the most dominant track record in recent F1 history. Mercedes' dominance between 2014-2020 was greater, and over a longer period of time, across driver lineups, than Red Bull's - in no small part because they got more out of both drivers. Over those seven seasons their drivers finished 1-2 in the driver's championship four times. Their average gap to the second placed constructors team was 52%. In only one year (2018) did their second driver finish outside the top three. Similarly, McLaren this year finished 78% over the second placed team in the constructors (833 / 469). Their two drivers finished 1-3. Red Bull, for all their success riding Verstappen's skill, only won two constructors titles between 2021-2024. The two years they failed, their second driver finished P4 (2021) and P8 (2024). Their gap to the second placed team in 2022 was only 37% (759 / 554). So, to answer your question directly: Why are we out here questioning a strategy that netted 4 WDC and 2 WCC? Because another strategy has yielded greater success, over a longer time frame. Teams like Alpine would love to be Red Bull, but I'm not sure teams like Mercedes or McLaren would.

text: The craziest thing is to know how insanely high the talent level is, and then to see people like Max somehow just be another tier above that. (Plenty of other names you could pick in history, but Max is a pretty obvious choice.) I've played some roller hockey in adult leagues at beginner skill levels. One day, a guy showed up to play for the opposing team, and he was wearing an Olympic jersey. It quickly became apparent that he might not have bought the jersey. I've never seen someone move like that - it can be frustrating when someone beats you, but this was like watching a magician up close. I didn't even know you could do some of the things he did. He wasn't running up the score, but he'd just effortlessly deke the puck between my legs and then make some ludicrous no-look pass. It wasn't even frustrating, it was just amazing to watch. Even so, this guy was a nobody compared to the bottom tier of the NHL. And the bottom tier of the NHL is a nobody compared to the all stars. It's hard to even imagine the talent ceiling, sometimes.
